# Transcrição: 03-Basic Finances for Business Owners

- **Fonte:** 03-Basic Finances for Business Owners
- **Duração:** 08:31
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:03]

Welcome back everybody. This is Greg Christiansen again for Mastermind Law Group. We're going to get into basic finances today. Now why would I do that? Understand that when I went to law school I had graduated from a four-year degree in finances. I was the only law student at the time that had a finance degree in my law school. And so what I found was pretty evident is you're dealing with a lot of kids that just graduated from college is they didn't understand basic financial principles. And so I spent a good portion of my time in law school kind of helping my fellow classmates and those around me to set some parameters to learn what it is. Because if you're like me, if you went and grew up in the United States, and I know a lot of you didn't grow up in the United States, but some of you did, if you went to standardized education courses and you didn't go to a private school to talk just finances, you probably didn't have a finance course in high school. You probably didn't have one in college or your graduate degrees. So where did you learn financial principles? A lot of us learn financial principles from the worst place possible, and that's our parents or our family members. And I can tell you I grew up in a home where there weren't really sound financial principles. And I decided I made a promise to myself that I was going to apply simple principles to learn how to be a good financial person, to have a good foundation because I never wanted to grow up or have my kids grow up in a home where their parents fought over finances. So we're going to talk about in this lesson is about how to put yourself in a good financial position without having to stress too much about it. What I've found is that most people hate the B word, and I'm not talking about the B word, I'm talking about the budget word. Most people don't really want to talk about how to figure out basic budgeting principles. So we're going to make it as simple as possible as we go through this training today. Think about it this way. Now a lot of you, and maybe you're not in the United States, maybe you're out, but you have equivalent type of coffee places you love to go to or the pub or wherever you like to go. I'm going to call it the Starbucks rule. I'm just going to pick this out because I have a lot of friends that love to go and get their lattes every morning. In fact, it's quite common in my office, one of my staff members comes in with a coffee that they say, hey, he wants to go to a Starbucks run or maybe it's Dutch brothers or maybe it's Dunkin Donuts or whatever it is. So what I've, I sat there and I asked him though, this the other day, I was like, how much are you spending right now on your average Starbucks run? And they're saying, well, it's, I don't know, they pulled out the receipts on average across the board. It was about 12 to $15 every time they went and got coffee from Starbucks. Now, is there a better use of our money in allocating that? So what I decided when I was graduating from high schools, maybe there's some simple approaches to budgeting you can implement. I went through and I made a simple list of all the expenses I had. I had a car and insurance and gas and food, basic foods, wanted to go on a date every once in a while. So I just listed that just big picture stuff, not granular.

## [03:03 - 06:08]

I made a spreadsheet and I walked through everything and I was catching every penny. I knew that I was, that was too strict for me. I wasn't going to be able to find it or follow that. So I went through and I made what I considered simple rules of budgeting. And you're going to laugh about this. The first thing I did is I said, I want to go to McDonald's once a week and I want to take my girlfriend out on a date every other week where we didn't stay home and watch a movie from blockbuster. And I know that dates myself. So I did that. And then what I did is I worked at a bank and when I got paid every two weeks of the time, this is a long time ago, so they didn't do an auto deposit. This was a check. I went and I put that check. I put it into a basic checking account at the bank I worked at. But I was like, Hey, if I've got cash in my pocket or I've got money in this account, you know what I'm going to do? I'm going to spend it. So I want to make sure that I don't spend everything. I want to be able to invest in myself. I want to pay for a college tuition without walking out of college with debt, student loans or anything else. I want to invest in my future. So I took whatever I needed those basic expenses, left it in the checking account, took everything else and I put it into a savings account in a separate institution. I didn't get a debit card. I didn't get withdrawal checks. I didn't get anything in there. I just got a savings account somewhere else. In fact, it was a branch of a credit union at the school in which I went to college. And I did that because they only had five branches at the time and it made it really difficult to go in and take money out of it. So I did that through the course of my college career. And what I found was is that a little bit every month, every week built up so I paid for my classes. I invested in myself. I paid off debt and I was able to be in a position of financial soundness. So when I graduated with my undergraduate degree, I didn't just have debt. I had money set aside. So let's go back to my Starbucks equation. So again, maybe you like going to Starbucks and you go, maybe it's 20 times a month. So I'm not saying get rid of Starbucks. Enjoy Starbucks. But just set some parameters. What if you only went a couple of times a week instead of every day of the week? What if you could just go ahead and set aside $100 a month that you regularly was spending on Starbucks? And I used the McDonald's example because recently there was an article that's been across the internet and on several news media outlets talking about how the Big Mac Mills at $18 a pop. And that's a lot of money. And so if you could just take the $100 that you're spending on fast food or Starbucks that you're just blowing right now, it's fun. It's good. But you just have a little bit of patience, a little bit of self-discipline. And you put that into a standard account where you're making the recent CD rate that I looked at yesterday was about 5%. Now, if you don't understand what a CD is, if you put money into that, it grows for six months and they pay you out the principal you put in, plus the interest at 5%. Say you did that for the next year. Say you did it for the next 10 years. You took $100 a month at 5%. You would

## [06:08 - 08:31]

go from having that first $100 investment to $15,000 in the bank after 10 years. You're like, wait, that's silly. That's the power of money. What I find with individuals who start businesses and knowledge business and any type of business really, that they don't set forth a foundation for them to be able to be successful because they don't understand how money works. And so they step into something not prepared foundationally to go ahead and succeed. So let me go through and talk about these few basic, basic principles. First and foremost, separate your expenses, the money you need to live on from the money that you have that's spending money. Take what you can allocate, take it out of that checking account, put it into a separate account that you can't touch. I do it in my business. I have an operating account. That's the account that I pay for all the employees and the lights and the utilities and all the rents that I pay. It's all into that one account. I take the rest of that. I put it into another account that's separate and distinct that I use for buying new equipment or hiring new staff when I want to expand. I use it to invest. I put it into investments. If you put these simple elements into place, I promise you, is you start making commercial decisions, investing decisions in the sense of consumer. You start to think like a business owner. You start having thoughts about creating foundations for the future. Try to find those things in your life, whether it's the Big Mac Mill or the Starbucks or the lattes you're getting at your local cafe or the pint at your local pub wherever you're at. Think about those things and see within the scope of what your current environment is, what can you save from and can you take and allocate that? And then you put it into two things. You put it into savings to do two things. One, to pay off debt and two, to invest in the future. Do I go into debt? Absolutely I do. But I have mechanisms in place to make sure that I can cover that debt and that I can save and build up my business in the future. If you implement this not only in your personal life, but as you start this knowledge based business, you're going to be in a foundation that's going to set you up for greater success because you understand how money works. Now, I understand we're barely scratching the surface on basic finances, but in the next course, we're going to go through and talk about business formations. So make sure you watch that because it's going to build on what we just discussed here.
